content.text: Every external call inside a tool handler must have a timeout. Without one, a single slow or unresponsive dependency can stall the entire agent turn indefinitely — blocking the model from producing a reply and eventually exhausting the platform's session budget. Timeouts transform an infinite wait into a predictable, recoverable error.

attrs.alt: Timeout layers in an agentic tool call — four nested rectangles: agent loop, connect, read, and statement timeouts

content.text: a connect timeout and a read timeout. A connect timeout alone does not protect against a server that accepts the connection and then sends nothing.
that sets both independently. A single number applies the same value to both, which is usually too generous for the connect phase.

import requests

# BAD: a single number is easy to get wrong
resp = requests.get("https://api.example.com/data", timeout=30)

# GOOD: explicit (connect_timeout, read_timeout) tuple
resp = requests.get(
    "https://api.example.com/data",
    timeout=(5, 15),   # 5 s to connect, 15 s to read the full response
)

# GOOD: for streaming endpoints where you read incrementally
resp = requests.get(
    "https://api.example.com/stream",
    timeout=(5, 0),    # 0 disables the per-chunk read timeout for streams
    stream=True,
)
Catching the right exceptions is equally important —
covers both phases, but you can distinguish them with
A timeout must produce a structured error dict, not a raw exception. The model needs enough information to decide whether to retry, to ask the user, or to give up gracefully.
in the response lets the caller distinguish a network-layer problem (connect) from a slow server (read), which may warrant different retry strategies.

Different tools have different latency profiles. A file-read tool should complete in milliseconds; a long-running report generation can legitimately take 30 seconds. Centralise the configuration so it is easy to review and adjust.

When an agent makes several tool calls in sequence, individual per-call timeouts can stack up to exceed the total deadline the user tolerates. Propagate a monotonic deadline from the outermost call into every tool invocation.
leaves a small margin for Python overhead and network RTT so the tool does not run right up to the deadline before erroring.
HTTP calls are not the only place timeouts matter. Database queries, subprocess executions, and file I/O can all block indefinitely.
— this runs inside the database engine and fires even if the Python process is stuck waiting for results.
SLA usually < 500 ms; 5 s is very generous
Short for OLTP; longer for analytics
Consider async: enqueue and poll instead

import pytest
import requests
from unittest.mock import patch
from requests.exceptions import ConnectTimeout, ReadTimeout

def test_connect_timeout_returns_structured_error():
    with patch("requests.get", side_effect=ConnectTimeout()):
        result = get_weather("London")
    assert result["error"] == "TIMEOUT"
    assert result["phase"] == "connect"
    assert "retry_after_seconds" in result

def test_read_timeout_returns_structured_error():
    with patch("requests.get", side_effect=ReadTimeout()):
        result = get_weather("London")
    assert result["error"] == "TIMEOUT"
    assert result["phase"] == "read"

def test_successful_response_parsed_correctly():
    mock_resp = requests.models.Response()
    mock_resp.status_code = 200
    mock_resp._content = b'{"temp_c": 18.5, "condition": "cloudy"}'
    with patch("requests.get", return_value=mock_resp):
        result = get_weather("London")
    assert result["temperature_c"] == 18.5
    assert result["city"] == "London"

def test_deadline_exceeded_before_call():
    import time
    past_deadline = time.monotonic() - 1  # already expired
    result = call_with_deadline(get_weather, "London", deadline=past_deadline)
    assert result["error"] == "TIMEOUT"
    assert "Deadline exceeded" in result["message"]
When tool handlers run inside an async framework (FastAPI, asyncio), use
timeout tuple and integrates cleanly with Python's
across all awaited calls inside it, so a single deadline guards the entire multi-step turn — no manual budget arithmetic required.
Communicating Timeout Errors to the Model
The model's retry behaviour depends entirely on what you tell it. A well-structured timeout response lets the model give the user an honest status update and decide whether to retry or escalate.
from the response only when you are certain the operation is idempotent and the model's system prompt already handles retry logic centrally.
Every tool call that touches a network, database, or subprocess needs explicit timeouts — never rely on OS or library defaults.
tuple rather than a single value; the connect phase should be much shorter than the read phase.
— never let raw exceptions propagate to the model.
For multi-step agents, propagate a monotonic deadline and cap each tool's read timeout to the remaining budget.
Database statement timeouts should be set at the DB engine level (e.g.
) in addition to the Python socket timeout.
Centralise per-tool timeout config in a
dict so latency budgets are easy to audit and tune.
